title: 🚑 ABCDE APPROACH
content:
Start with the unstable patient, not the final diagnosis.

A Airway
• Can the patient speak
• Look for obstruction
• Protect cervical spine if trauma

B Breathing
• Respiratory rate
• Oxygen saturation
• Chest rise
• Breath sounds

C Circulation
• Pulse and blood pressure
• Capillary refill
• Active bleeding
• IV access

D Disability
• GCS
• Pupils
• Blood glucose

E Exposure
• Full body exam
• Look for hidden injuries
• Prevent hypothermia


Quick Flow


StepMain focusImmediate action
APatent airwaySuction, airway maneuver
BOxygenationOxygen, auscultation
CPerfusionIV access, fluids, stop bleeding
DNeurologic statusGCS, pupils, glucose
EFull inspectionExpose, keep warm

quiz.q: What is the first priority in any unstable trauma patient?

title: 🩸 SHOCK OVERVIEW
content:
Shock means inadequate tissue perfusion.

Hypovolemic shock
• Hemorrhage or fluid loss
• Cold, clammy skin
• Tachycardia

Septic shock
• Infection source
• Warm extremities early
• Vasodilation
• High lactate

Cardiogenic shock
• Pump failure
• Often after MI
• Pulmonary edema may coexist

Obstructive shock
• PE
• Cardiac tamponade
• Tension pneumothorax

Exam Memory Map


TypeSkinMain clue
HypovolemicColdBleeding or severe fluid loss
SepticWarm earlyInfection + hypotension
CardiogenicColdMI or pump failure
ObstructiveColdMechanical blockage of circulation

quiz.q: Which type of shock typically has warm extremities early?

title: 🌬️ TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX
content:
This is an immediate life threat.

Classic findings
• Sudden severe dyspnea
• Hypotension
• Reduced breath sounds on one side
• Tracheal deviation
• Distended neck veins may appear

Immediate treatment
• Needle decompression first
• Chest tube after stabilization

One-minute diagram


FeatureTension pneumothorax
Breath soundsReduced on affected side
Blood pressureLow
Neck veinsMay be distended
ActionNeedle decompression now

quiz.q: Should you wait for a chest X-ray if tension pneumothorax is clinically obvious?

title: 🦠 SEPSIS BUNDLE
content:
Suspect sepsis when infection is followed by organ dysfunction or shock.

Red flags
• Hypotension
• Altered mental status
• Tachycardia
• Fever or hypothermia
• High lactate

Early management
• ABCs
• Blood cultures if possible
• Broad-spectrum antibiotics early
• IV crystalloids
• Repeat perfusion assessment

Sepsis flowchart


StepWhy it matters
Antibiotics earlyReduces mortality
FluidsImproves perfusion
LactateMarker of hypoperfusion
ReassessmentGuides vasopressors and escalation

quiz.q: What is one of the most time-sensitive treatments in sepsis?

title: 🍬 DKA VS HYPOGLYCEMIA
content:
Two exam favorites that must be separated fast.

FeatureDKAHypoglycemia
GlucoseHighLow
Mental statusConfused or drowsyConfused, sweaty, shaky
BreathingKussmaul breathing may occurUsually no acidotic breathing
KetonesPresentAbsent
Immediate treatmentIV fluids + insulin + potassium monitoringGive glucose now

Decision pathway

quiz.q: What must be monitored closely after starting insulin in DKA?

title: ⚡ CARDIAC ARREST RHYTHMS
content:
Four rhythms must be recognized immediately in emergency medicine.

Shockable rhythms
• Ventricular fibrillation
• Pulseless ventricular tachycardia

Non-shockable rhythms
• Asystole
• Pulseless electrical activity

RhythmShockableMain treatment
Ventricular fibrillationYesDefibrillation + CPR
Pulseless VTYesDefibrillation + CPR
AsystoleNoCPR + Epinephrine
PEANoCPR + Treat reversible cause

Rapid logic

quiz.q: Which cardiac arrest rhythms require defibrillation?
quiz.a: Ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia.

title: ☠️ OPIOID OVERDOSE
content:
Classic opioid toxicity must be recognized rapidly.

Classic triad
• Decreased consciousness
• Respiratory depression
• Pinpoint pupils

FindingMeaning
Slow breathingRespiratory center suppression
MiosisOpioid effect
Low consciousnessCNS depression

Treatment
• Naloxone
• Airway support
• Oxygen

Decision strip

quiz.q: What medication reverses opioid overdose?

title: 🧠 STROKE EMERGENCY
content:
Time is brain. Early treatment improves outcome.

Common symptoms
• Sudden weakness
• Speech difficulty
• Facial droop
• Vision loss

FASTMeaning
FaceFacial droop
ArmArm weakness
SpeechSpeech difficulty
TimeEmergency response now

Key step
Immediate CT scan to rule out hemorrhage.

Stroke pathway

quiz.q: What imaging must be done first in suspected stroke?

title: 🫁 ACUTE ASTHMA ATTACK
content:
Acute asthma exacerbation presents with airway narrowing.

Symptoms
• Wheezing
• Dyspnea
• Chest tightness
• Cough

SeverityTypical finding
MildSpeaks full sentences
ModerateSpeaks phrases
SevereSpeaks single words or cannot complete sentences

Treatment
• Oxygen if needed
• Short acting beta agonist
• Steroids

Severity logic

quiz.q: What is the first bronchodilator used in acute asthma?
quiz.a: Short acting beta agonist such as salbutamol.

title: ⚠️ ANAPHYLAXIS
content:
A life-threatening systemic allergic reaction.

Common triggers
• Food
• Drugs
• Insect stings

SystemSymptoms
SkinUrticaria, itching
RespiratoryWheezing, throat swelling
CardiovascularHypotension

Immediate treatment
Intramuscular epinephrine.

Emergency flow

quiz.q: What is the first line treatment for anaphylaxis?

title: ❤️ ACUTE MI
content:
Think acute myocardial infarction in any high-risk patient with ischemic chest pain.

Classic symptoms
• Crushing chest pain
• Radiation to arm or jaw
• Sweating
• Nausea
• Dyspnea

Immediate stepReason
ECGLook for STEMI/NSTEMI pattern
TroponinDetect myocardial injury
AspirinEarly antiplatelet effect
Reperfusion if STEMIRestore blood flow

MI sequence

quiz.q: What is the first investigation in suspected acute MI?

title: 💥 AORTIC DISSECTION
content:
This is one of the most dangerous causes of chest pain.

Classic clue
Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back.

Other clues
• Hypertension history
• Pulse deficit
• BP difference between arms
• New aortic regurgitation murmur may occur

FeatureDissection clue
PainTearing, abrupt onset
RadiationTo back
VitalsMay have severe hypertension or shock
ActionUrgent imaging and blood pressure control

Quick differentiator

quiz.q: What pain description is classic for aortic dissection?
quiz.a: Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back.

title: 🫀 PULMONARY EMBOLISM
content:
Pulmonary embolism must be considered in unexplained sudden dyspnea.

Typical features
• Sudden shortness of breath
• Pleuritic chest pain
• Tachycardia
• Hypoxia

Risk factors
• Immobility
• Recent surgery
• Cancer
• Pregnancy
• Previous DVT/PE

Clinical clueMeaning
Sudden dyspneaCommon presentation
Pleuritic painLung infarct or pleural irritation
TachycardiaFrequent sign
Risk factors presentRaises probability

PE thought path

quiz.q: What imaging is commonly used to confirm pulmonary embolism?

title: 🩸 UPPER GI BLEED
content:
Upper GI bleeding is an emergency when hemodynamic instability is present.

Key findings
• Hematemesis
• Melena
• Dizziness or syncope
• Hypotension if severe

Immediate priorities
• ABCs
• Large-bore IV access
• Fluids and blood if needed
• PPI
• Endoscopy once stabilized

FeatureInterpretation
HematemesisUpper GI source likely
MelenaDigested blood
Shock signsSevere blood loss
ManagementResuscitate before scope

Resuscitation flow

quiz.q: What comes first in an unstable upper GI bleed, endoscopy or resuscitation?

title: ⚠️ STATUS EPILEPTICUS
content:
Status epilepticus is a true emergency because prolonged seizures injure the brain.

Definition
Seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without return to baseline.

First treatment
• Benzodiazepine such as lorazepam
• Then second-line anti-seizure therapy if needed

StepTreatment
FirstAirway, oxygen, glucose check
SecondLorazepam
ThirdLonger-acting anti-seizure medication

Emergency sequence

quiz.q: What is the first medication class used in status epilepticus?

title: 📉 HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY
content:
Hypertensive emergency is not defined by the number alone. It is severe blood pressure elevation with acute end-organ damage.

Target organ damage
• Encephalopathy
• Acute pulmonary edema
• Aortic dissection
• Acute kidney injury
• Retinal hemorrhage or papilledema

SituationMeaning
Very high BP without damageUrgency, not emergency
Very high BP with organ injuryEmergency
Neurologic symptomsThink brain involvement
Pulmonary edemaThink cardiac strain

Core concept

quiz.q: What makes severe hypertension an emergency rather than only urgency?

title: 💥 SUBARACHNOID HEMORRHAGE
content:
Subarachnoid hemorrhage is a classic cause of sudden severe headache and must never be missed.

Classic clue
Worst headache of life or thunderclap headache.

Associated features
• Neck stiffness
• Vomiting
• Photophobia
• Reduced consciousness

StepPurpose
CT headFirst investigation
Lumbar puncture if CT negative and suspicion remainsLook for xanthochromia
Neurosurgical assessmentDefinitive management planning

SAH pathway

quiz.q: What headache description is classic for subarachnoid hemorrhage?
quiz.a: Worst headache of life or thunderclap headache.

title: 🚗 TRAUMA PRIMARY SURVEY
content:
In trauma, the first survey is always about immediate threats to life.

Primary survey
• Airway with cervical spine protection
• Breathing
• Circulation with hemorrhage control
• Disability
• Exposure

LetterMain task
AProtect airway and neck
BCheck chest movement and breath sounds
CStop bleeding and restore perfusion
DAssess GCS and pupils
EExpose fully and prevent hypothermia

Trauma flow

quiz.q: What comes first in trauma, CT scan or primary survey?

title: 🩸 FAST ULTRASOUND
content:
FAST is a bedside trauma ultrasound used to detect free fluid rapidly in unstable trauma patients.

Typical uses
• Blunt abdominal trauma
• Hemodynamic instability
• Quick search for internal bleeding

AreaWhy checked
PericardiumTamponade
Right upper quadrantFree intraperitoneal fluid
Left upper quadrantSplenic area bleeding
PelvisDependent free fluid

FAST zones

quiz.q: What is FAST used for in trauma?
quiz.a: Rapid bedside detection of internal free fluid.

title: 🫀 CARDIAC TAMPONADE VS TENSION PTX
content:
These two both cause obstructive shock and can look similar in trauma.

FeatureTamponadeTension PTX
Breath soundsUsually present bilaterallyReduced on one side
Heart soundsMuffledNot classic
JVPRaisedRaised may occur
TracheaUsually centralMay deviate
Immediate treatmentDrain pericardiumNeedle decompression

Exam pearl
Unilateral absent breath sounds strongly support tension pneumothorax.

quiz.q: What finding strongly favors tension pneumothorax over tamponade?
quiz.a: Unilateral reduced or absent breath sounds.

title: 🔥 BURN CLASSIFICATION
content:
Burn questions in MCCQE often test depth and next-step management.

DepthSkin findingPain
First degreeRed, dryPainful
Second degreeBlisters, moistVery painful
Third degreeWhite, leathery, charredMay be less painful due to nerve destruction

High-yield point
Third degree burns are full-thickness burns.

quiz.q: Which burn class typically has blisters?

title: 💧 BURN RESUSCITATION
content:
Large burns require aggressive fluid resuscitation because of capillary leak and fluid loss.

Key principle
Use crystalloid resuscitation and monitor urine output.

Burn issueWhy it matters
Large TBSA burnMajor fluid loss
Urine outputGuides adequacy of resuscitation
Airway burn riskMay need early airway control

Exam pearl
Think inhalation injury if burn occurred in closed space with facial burns or soot.

quiz.q: What parameter is commonly used to monitor adequacy of burn fluid resuscitation?

title: 🌡️ HEAT STROKE
content:
Heat stroke is hyperthermia with central nervous system dysfunction and is an emergency.

Core features
• Very high temperature
• Altered mental status
• Hot skin

Immediate treatment
Rapid cooling and supportive care.

ConditionKey difference
Heat exhaustionNo major CNS dysfunction
Heat strokeAltered mental state present

quiz.q: What distinguishes heat stroke from heat exhaustion?
quiz.a: Central nervous system dysfunction such as confusion or coma.

title: 🥶 HYPOTHERMIA
content:
Hypothermia is core temperature below 35°C and can affect every organ system.

Common findings
• Shivering early
• Confusion
• Bradycardia
• Slow reflexes

Management
• Gentle handling
• Rewarming
• Monitor for arrhythmias

SeverityGeneral approach
MildPassive external warming
Moderate to severeActive rewarming and monitoring

quiz.q: What core temperature defines hypothermia?

title: 💊 ACETAMINOPHEN OVERDOSE
content:
Acetaminophen overdose is common and dangerous because liver failure may develop after an initial well-looking period.

Key point
Do not be reassured by the absence of early symptoms.

Antidote
N-acetylcysteine.

IssueWhy important
Early symptoms may be minimalToxicity can still be severe
Liver injury riskMain life-threatening complication
N-acetylcysteineProtects the liver if given appropriately

quiz.q: What is the antidote for acetaminophen overdose?

title: 🧪 ORGANOPHOSPHATE POISONING
content:
Organophosphate poisoning causes cholinergic excess.

Classic signs
• Salivation
• Lacrimation
• Urination
• Diarrhea
• Bronchospasm
• Bradycardia

Treatment
• Atropine
• Pralidoxime in appropriate settings

FeatureDirection
SecretionsIncreased
PupilsSmall
LungsWet, bronchospasm possible
Heart rateMay be slow

quiz.q: What is the main first antidote used for organophosphate poisoning?

title: 🚬 CARBON MONOXIDE POISONING
content:
Carbon monoxide poisoning should be suspected when headache, dizziness, and confusion occur in enclosed-space exposure.

Common clues
• Headache
• Dizziness
• Nausea
• Confusion
• Multiple people affected in same place

Treatment
High-flow oxygen.

ClueWhy it matters
Enclosed space heater or fire exposureStrong source clue
Normal pulse oximetry can misleadIt may not detect CO burden properly
Oxygen therapyMain treatment

quiz.q: What is the first treatment for carbon monoxide poisoning?

title: 👶 CHILD DEHYDRATION
content:
Pediatric dehydration is common in emergency settings and severity assessment matters.

Signs of dehydration
• Dry mucosa
• Reduced tears
• Sunken eyes
• Decreased urine output
• Lethargy if severe

Severity clueInterpretation
Drinking well, alertMild
Sunken eyes, poor urine outputModerate
Lethargy, poor perfusionSevere

Management
Oral rehydration solution is first-line in mild to moderate dehydration if the child can drink.

quiz.q: What is first-line treatment for mild to moderate dehydration in a child who can drink?

title: 🤒 FEBRILE SEIZURE
content:
A febrile seizure usually occurs in young children with fever and no CNS infection.

Simple febrile seizure features
• Generalized
• Lasts less than 15 minutes
• Does not recur within 24 hours

SimpleComplex
GeneralizedFocal features may occur
Short durationLonger duration
Single episode in 24 hMay recur in 24 h

Key management
Reassure, control fever source, and look for red flags of meningitis or other serious illness.

quiz.q: What is one feature of a simple febrile seizure?
quiz.a: It is generalized and short, typically under 15 minutes.

title: 🤰 ECLAMPSIA
content:
Eclampsia means seizures in a patient with preeclampsia and is a life-threatening obstetric emergency.

Core clues
• Hypertension in pregnancy
• Proteinuria may be present
• Seizure

Immediate treatment
Magnesium sulfate and urgent obstetric management.

ConditionMain idea
PreeclampsiaHypertension after 20 weeks with organ involvement/proteinuria
EclampsiaPreeclampsia plus seizure

quiz.q: What is the first-line medication used to prevent and treat seizures in eclampsia?

title: ❤️ CHEST PAIN: CANADIAN ED SORT
content:
In MCCQE style questions, start by ruling out life threats before benign causes.

Red diagnosisClassic clueFirst key step
ACSPressure, diaphoresis, radiationECG + troponin
Aortic dissectionTearing pain to back, pulse deficitUrgent imaging + BP control
PESudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, tachycardiaAssess probability, then D-dimer/CTPA
Tension PTXUnilateral absent breath sounds, shockImmediate decompression
PericarditisWorse lying flat, better leaning forwardECG + anti-inflammatory approach

Canadian exam shortcut
Chest pain + unstable vitals = resuscitate first
Do not jump to GERD until lethal causes are excluded

Mnemonic
MAP-T = MI, Aortic dissection, PE, Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade

quiz.q: What must be ruled out first in any undifferentiated acute chest pain in the ED?
quiz.a: Immediately life-threatening causes such as ACS, aortic dissection, PE, tension pneumothorax, and tamponade.

title: ⚡ ACS: STEMI VS NSTEMI VS UA
content:
Acute coronary syndrome is a Canadian exam favorite.

TypeECGTroponinCore management
STEMIST elevationUsually elevatedImmediate reperfusion
NSTEMINo ST elevationElevatedAntiplatelet + anticoagulation + risk stratify
Unstable anginaNo ST elevationNormalTreat as ACS, no biomarker rise

STEMI = artery fully occluded until proven otherwise
First investigations
• ECG within minutes
• Serial troponins if needed
• Repeat ECG if pain continues

High-yield sequence
Chest pain → ECG → STEMI? → reperfuse
No STEMI but suspicious → serial ECG/troponin

quiz.q: What distinguishes NSTEMI from unstable angina?
quiz.a: Troponin elevation in NSTEMI, with no troponin rise in unstable angina.

title: 🫁 ACUTE DYSPNEA DIFFERENTIAL
content:
Use pattern recognition fast in MCCQE stems.

DiagnosisBest clueImmediate move
AsthmaWheeze, previous episodes, triggerSABA + steroids
COPD exacerbationSmoker, increased sputum/dyspneaControlled O2 + bronchodilator + steroids
PEPleuritic pain, risk factors, tachycardiaProbability-based workup
PneumothoraxSudden unilateral pleuritic painIf tension, decompress now
CHF/pulmonary edemaOrthopnea, crackles, edemaO2, nitrates/diuresis if appropriate
PneumoniaFever, cough, focal cracklesAntibiotics if bacterial

ABG pearl
COPD + chronic high CO2 + near-normal pH = compensated respiratory acidosis
Silent chest in asthma = severe attack

quiz.q: What physical finding in asthma suggests a severe attack rather than mild wheeze?
quiz.a: A silent chest or inability to speak full sentences.

title: 🧠 STROKE / TIA: CANADIAN NEXT STEP
content:
Stroke questions often test first investigation and immediate reasoning.

ScenarioMeaningBest next step
Sudden focal deficit nowAcute strokeNon-contrast CT head
Symptoms resolved in 30 minTIAUrgent vascular/cardiac evaluation
Weakness + aphasiaAnterior circulationCT first, then treat by type
Thunderclap headacheThink SAHCT head ± LP if needed

CT first because hemorrhage changes management
Do not label as migraine without excluding stroke in focal deficits
FAST
Face
Arm
Speech
Time

quiz.q: What is the first imaging test in suspected acute stroke?
quiz.a: A non-contrast CT scan of the head.

title: 🍬 DKA VS HHS
content:
Differentiate diabetic emergencies quickly.

FeatureDKAHHS
Typical patientType 1, youngerType 2, older
KetonesPresentMinimal or absent
AcidosisMarkedMild/absent
GlucoseHighVery high
DehydrationModerate-severeSevere

Initial management sequence
1. IV fluids
2. Check potassium
3. Start insulin when safe
4. Treat trigger

Potassium kills faster than glucose
Always monitor K after insulin starts

quiz.q: What is the first treatment step in DKA?

title: 🧂 SODIUM EMERGENCIES
content:
Sodium questions often test pace and danger of correction.

DisorderClueMajor danger
HyponatremiaConfusion, seizures if severeCerebral edema
HypernatremiaThirst, confusion, dehydrationBrain shrinkage / bleeding risk

Severe symptomatic hyponatremia may need hypertonic saline
Correct chronic hyponatremia too fast = osmotic demyelination
Correct hypernatremia too fast = cerebral edema

quiz.q: What neurologic complication can occur if chronic hyponatremia is corrected too quickly?

title: 🚽 AKI: PRE-RENAL VS INTRINSIC VS POST-RENAL
content:
Acute kidney injury is organized by location of the problem.

TypeCauseCommon clue
Pre-renalLow perfusionHypovolemia, CHF, sepsis
IntrinsicKidney tissue injuryATN, GN, AIN
Post-renalObstructionBPH, stones, pelvic mass

Post-renal causes must be excluded fast because they may be reversible
Think obstruction in elderly men with urinary symptoms

quiz.q: What category of AKI should be suspected in an older man with oliguria and a distended bladder?
quiz.a: Post-renal AKI due to urinary obstruction.

title: 🩸 GI BLEED: UPPER VS LOWER
content:
GI bleed questions focus on pattern, resuscitation, and source localization.

FeatureUpper GI bleedLower GI bleed
Typical stoolMelenaHematochezia
Vomiting bloodPossibleAbsent
Initial priorityResuscitationResuscitation
Main procedureEndoscopyColonoscopy once stable

Massive upper bleed can also present with hematochezia
Always stabilize before scope

quiz.q: What comes first in a hemodynamically unstable GI bleed?
quiz.a: Resuscitation with airway, IV access, and circulatory support.

title: 🪨 ACUTE ABDOMEN PATTERNS
content:
Pattern recognition saves time in Canadian exam stems.

Pain patternThinkPearl
Epigastric to backPancreatitisCheck lipase
RLQ + feverAppendicitisLocalized tenderness
RUQ after fatty mealBiliary diseaseMurphy sign may be present
Pain out of proportionMesenteric ischemiaVascular emergency
Rigid abdomenPeritonitis / perforationSurgical abdomen

Do not send surgical abdomen home with simple analgesia only

quiz.q: What diagnosis is suggested by severe abdominal pain out of proportion to physical exam?

title: 🤰 PREGNANCY BLEEDING PATTERNS
content:
MCCQE loves painless versus painful bleeding distinctions.

ScenarioThinkPearl
1st trimester pain + bleedingEctopic or miscarriageRule out ectopic first
Painless late pregnancy bleedingPlacenta previaNo digital exam until location known
Painful bleeding + tender uterusAbruptionMaternal stabilization first
Postpartum hemorrhage + boggy uterusUterine atonyMassage + uterotonics
Postpartum bleed + firm uterusLaceration/coagulopathyLook for trauma

quiz.q: What diagnosis is classically associated with painless vaginal bleeding in late pregnancy?

title: ⚠️ PRE-ECLAMPSIA / ECLAMPSIA
content:
Hypertension in pregnancy after 20 weeks is never a casual finding.

ConditionKey featuresHigh-yield treatment
PreeclampsiaHTN + proteinuria or organ dysfunctionAssess severity, maternal/fetal monitoring
EclampsiaPreeclampsia + seizureMagnesium sulfate
HELLPHemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low plateletsUrgent obstetric management

Magnesium is for seizure prevention/treatment, not BP control

quiz.q: What is the first-line medication for seizure control and prevention in eclampsia?

title: 🧷 CONTRACEPTION: CANADIAN EXAM PICKS
content:
Canadian contraception questions often ask which method fits the comorbidity.

ScenarioPreferred idea
Migraine with auraAvoid estrogen-containing contraception
Heavy menstrual bleedingLevonorgestrel IUD is high-yield
Postpartum breastfeedingIUD or progestin-only options often suitable
Inherited thrombophilia riskAvoid estrogen if contraindicated

LNG-IUD is repeatedly favored in exam cases with menorrhagia

quiz.q: What contraceptive category should generally be avoided in migraine with aura?

title: 👶 PEDIATRIC RESPIRATORY TRIAD
content:
Three pediatric respiratory patterns recur constantly.

DiagnosisBest clueUsual management
CroupBarking cough + stridorDexamethasone ± nebulized epinephrine
EpiglottitisDrooling + toxic child + soft stridorSecure airway
BronchiolitisInfant + wheeze after viral prodromeSupportive care

Do not agitate suspected epiglottitis
Bronchiolitis does not usually need routine bronchodilator trials on exam questions unless specified

quiz.q: What diagnosis should be suspected in a drooling child with fever and stridor?

title: 🧒 PEDS GI FAVORITES
content:
These are repeated pediatric GI diagnosis traps.

DiagnosisClassic clueBest step
Pyloric stenosisProjectile non-bilious vomitingConfirm and rehydrate before surgery
IntussusceptionIntermittent pain + currant jelly stoolUrgent reduction if stable
Hirschsprung diseaseDelayed meconium + constipationConfirm aganglionosis
Meckel diverticulumPainless rectal bleedingThink ectopic gastric tissue

quiz.q: What diagnosis is suggested by intermittent colicky abdominal pain and currant jelly stool in a child?

title: 🌞 NEONATAL JAUNDICE SORT
content:
Neonatal jaundice questions are pattern-based.

PatternThink
Day 3 indirect bilirubin, well babyPhysiologic jaundice
Early jaundice, hemolysis cluesABO/Rh incompatibility or other hemolysis
Poor feeding, dehydrationBreastfeeding jaundice
Persistent indirect jaundice in thriving infantBreast milk jaundice
Pale stool + dark urine + direct bilirubinBiliary atresia

Direct hyperbilirubinemia is never physiologic

quiz.q: What diagnosis is suggested by neonatal jaundice with pale stools and dark urine?
quiz.a: Biliary atresia or another cause of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.

title: 🧠 PSYCH EMERGENCY FIRST QUESTION
content:
In depressive and psychiatric presentations, the first life-saving question is often about self-harm risk.

PresentationDo not miss
DepressionAsk about suicidal ideation, plan, intent
PsychosisRisk to self or others
AgitationMedical cause, intoxication, delirium
Anxiety in EDStill do full history and physical

Canadian exam rule = assess safety before diagnosis refinement

quiz.q: What is the most important first question in a patient presenting with depressive symptoms?
quiz.a: Ask about suicidal ideation and self-harm risk.

title: 😞 DEPRESSION VS GRIEF VS ADJUSTMENT
content:
These are heavily tested distinctions.

ConditionCore clueUsual approach
MDDPersistent low mood/anhedonia with functional impairmentPsychotherapy and/or antidepressants
GriefLoss-focused distress, usually waves, preserved self-worthSupportive management
Adjustment disorderStress-linked symptoms out of proportion, time limitedPsychotherapy first

Suicidality, psychosis, or severe functional collapse shifts concern toward major depression or emergency care

quiz.q: What diagnosis is suggested by emotional symptoms occurring within months of a stressor and out of proportion to it, but not meeting criteria for major depression?

title: 📈 MANIA / BIPOLAR
content:
Mania is recognized by activation plus impaired judgment.

Classic symptomWhy it matters
Decreased need for sleepHigh-yield clue
GrandiosityDistinguishes from normal energy
Risky behaviorOften drives presentation
Pressured speechThought acceleration

Avoid starting antidepressant monotherapy in active mania
Acute agitation may need antipsychotic or benzodiazepine support

quiz.q: What sleep pattern is highly suggestive of mania?
quiz.a: A markedly decreased need for sleep.

title: 🧩 PSYCHOSIS DIFFERENTIATOR
content:
Different psychotic disorders are separated by time course and mood relationship.

DiagnosisBest clue
Brief psychotic disorderLess than 1 month
Schizophreniform1 to 6 months
SchizophreniaMore than 6 months
Schizoaffective disorderPsychosis persists for at least 2 weeks without mood symptoms
Delusional disorderFixed delusion, otherwise relatively preserved functioning

quiz.q: What diagnosis is suggested by fixed non-bizarre delusions with otherwise preserved functioning?

title: 🌪️ DELIRIUM VS DEMENTIA
content:
This is one of the highest-yield elderly distinctions.

FeatureDeliriumDementia
OnsetAcuteGradual
CourseFluctuatingProgressive
AttentionImpairedOften preserved early
ConsciousnessAlteredUsually clear until late
CauseUsually medical triggerNeurodegenerative or chronic

Delirium is a medical emergency until proven otherwise

quiz.q: What cognitive feature most strongly favors delirium over dementia?
quiz.a: Acute fluctuating inattention with altered level of consciousness.

title: 🧪 SCREENING TEST PEARLS
content:
MCCQE often asks what screening tests can and cannot tell you.

MetricMeaning
SensitivityAbility to detect disease when disease is present
SpecificityAbility to exclude disease when disease is absent
PPVProbability disease is present if test is positive
NPVProbability disease is absent if test is negative

Low PPV means a positive screening test does not confirm diagnosis
A negative screen with low score still requires proper clinical assessment if symptoms matter

quiz.q: What does a low positive predictive value mean?
quiz.a: A positive test result is not strongly reliable for confirming disease.

title: 📊 STUDY TYPES & EPIDEMIOLOGY
content:
Canadian exams repeatedly test study design recognition.

Study typeBest use
Cross-sectionalPrevalence at one point in time
CohortFollow exposure to outcome
Case-controlRare disease, look backward for exposure
RCTIntervention effectiveness
Descriptive observationalDescribe patterns, generate hypotheses

Prevalence = how many have it now
Incidence = new cases over time

quiz.q: What study design is best for measuring prevalence in a community?

title: ⚖️ CANADIAN ETHICS: FIRST MOVES
content:
Ethics questions usually test confidentiality, consent, capacity, and respectful process.

ScenarioBest first move
Police ask for patient infoDiscuss request with patient unless mandatory exception applies
Patient complaint about another physicianSupport patient and follow reporting duties if required
Adult refuses treatment with capacityRespect refusal
Capacity unclear in acute illnessTreat reversible causes, reassess capacity
Emergency and no consent possibleTreat life-threatening condition

Capacity is decision-specific and can change over time

quiz.q: What is usually the best first step when police request confidential patient information in a non-mandatory scenario?
quiz.a: Contact the patient to discuss the request.

title: 🧬 INFERTILITY WORKUP ORDER
content:
Infertility workup questions often test sequence, not obscure causes.

First-line domainExample
Male factorSemen analysis
OvulationCycle history, ovulation evidence
Tubal/uterine factorImaging when indicated

High-yield exam pearl = do not skip semen analysis
Obesity and irregular menses increase suspicion for ovulatory dysfunction, but male factor still needs assessment

quiz.q: What is one of the first investigations in an infertility workup?

title: 🫀 VALVULAR RED FLAGS
content:
Only a few murmur patterns are repeatedly tested in emergency and internal medicine stems.

Valve lesionClassic clue
Aortic stenosisSyncope, angina, dyspnea; systolic murmur radiates to carotids
Tricuspid regurgitationHolosystolic murmur at lower left sternal border with right heart failure signs
Aortic regurgitationDiastolic murmur with wide pulse pressure
Mitral stenosisOpening snap and diastolic rumble

Exertional syncope should always raise concern for aortic stenosis

quiz.q: What murmur radiation is classically associated with aortic stenosis?

title: 🦵 ACUTE HOT JOINT
content:
A hot swollen joint with systemic symptoms is septic arthritis until proven otherwise.

DiagnosisKey cluePriority
Septic arthritisFever + painful immobile jointUrgent aspiration and treatment
GoutAcute monoarthritis, often big toeCrystal diagnosis if needed
OAChronic mechanical painNot a septic picture

Never miss septic arthritis because delay destroys joint cartilage

quiz.q: What is the most urgent diagnosis to exclude in a febrile patient with an acutely swollen, painful joint?

title: 🩸 ANEMIA SORT
content:
Canadian questions often reduce anemia to MCV patterns plus key clinical clues.

MCV patternCommon causes
MicrocyticIron deficiency, thalassemia, chronic disease
NormocyticAcute blood loss, chronic disease, hemolysis
MacrocyticB12 deficiency, folate deficiency, liver disease

Heavy menstrual bleeding is a classic iron deficiency stem
Neurologic findings point toward B12 deficiency

quiz.q: What deficiency should be suspected in macrocytic anemia with neurologic findings?

title: 🫁 PNEUMONIA QUICK SORT
content:
Do not overcomplicate common pneumonia stems.

TypeClue
Typical CAPFever, productive cough, focal consolidation
Atypical pneumoniaDry cough, diffuse findings, milder auscultation mismatch
AspirationRisk of impaired consciousness or swallowing

Mycoplasma is a classic atypical pathogen in adolescents/young adults

quiz.q: What pathogen is classically associated with atypical pneumonia in a teenager with dry cough and diffuse chest findings?

title: 🧓 DEMENTIA PEARLS
content:
Dementia questions often focus on type patterns and functional assessment.

TypeClassic clue
Alzheimer diseaseGradual memory decline
Lewy body dementiaVisual hallucinations + parkinsonism + fluctuating cognition
Normal-pressure hydrocephalusGait change + incontinence + cognitive decline
Vascular dementiaStepwise decline

Do not decide discharge disposition during acute delirium as if it were baseline dementia

quiz.q: What triad is classic for normal-pressure hydrocephalus?
quiz.a: Gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline.

title: 🇨🇦 MCCQE CANADIAN EXAM TRAPS
content:
TrapCorrect exam thought
Worst headache of lifeSAH until proven otherwise
Painless hematuria in smokerBladder cancer until proven otherwise
Sudden testicular painTorsion until proven otherwise
Drooling child with feverEpiglottitis, airway first
Irregularly irregular pulseAtrial fibrillation
MelenaUpper GI source usually
Painless jaundice + weight lossPancreatic cancer concern
Pain out of proportion in abdomenMesenteric ischemia
Thunderclap headacheCT first, then LP if needed
Police ask for recordsThink confidentiality and patient discussion first

Use this block for fast pre-exam review

quiz.q: What is the classic MCCQE interpretation of sudden painless hematuria in an older smoker?

title: 🫀 CHEST PAIN RED FLAGS
content:
In MCCQE, chest pain means rule out deadly causes first.

Do not miss
• Acute coronary syndrome
• Aortic dissection
• Pulmonary embolism
• Tension pneumothorax
• Cardiac tamponade
• Esophageal rupture

DiagnosisClassic clueImmediate action
ACSPressure-like pain, diaphoresisECG and troponin
DissectionTearing pain to backUrgent imaging and BP control
PEPleuritic pain, dyspneaRisk stratify and image
Tension PTXUnilateral absent breath soundsNeedle decompression
TamponadeHypotension, raised JVPUrgent drainage
BoerhaaveVomiting then severe chest painUrgent surgical review

Exam order

quiz.q: What are the first life-threatening causes to exclude in chest pain?
quiz.a: ACS, aortic dissection, PE, tension pneumothorax, tamponade, and esophageal rupture.

title: 📈 STEMI VS NSTEMI VS UA
content:
Acute coronary syndromes differ by ECG and troponin pattern.

ConditionECGTroponinMain idea
STEMIST elevationPositiveFull thickness infarction, urgent reperfusion
NSTEMINo ST elevationPositiveMyocardial necrosis without STEMI pattern
Unstable anginaNo ST elevationNegativeIschemia without infarction

STEMI action
Do not delay reperfusion.

All ACS patients
• ECG early
• Aspirin unless contraindicated
• Risk stratify
• Monitor and escalate

Pattern logic

quiz.q: How do you distinguish NSTEMI from unstable angina?
quiz.a: NSTEMI has positive troponins, while unstable angina does not.

title: 🫁 ACUTE PULMONARY EDEMA
content:
Acute pulmonary edema causes respiratory distress due to fluid in the lungs.

Clues
• Severe dyspnea
• Orthopnea
• Crackles
• Hypoxia
• Pink frothy sputum may occur

Immediate management
• Sit upright
• Oxygen or ventilatory support if needed
• Treat precipitating cause
• Diuresis when appropriate

FindingInterpretation
CracklesAlveolar fluid
HypertensionOften trigger or association
JVP raisedVolume overload or heart failure
HypoxiaGas exchange failure

Emergency path

quiz.q: What bedside finding strongly supports pulmonary edema?
quiz.a: Diffuse crackles in a patient with acute dyspnea and hypoxia.

title: 🧠 RAISED ICP
content:
Raised intracranial pressure is a dangerous emergency because it threatens brain perfusion and can cause herniation.

Red flags
• Reduced level of consciousness
• Vomiting
• Severe headache
• Papilledema
• Focal neurologic deficit
• Cushing response may appear late

ConcernWhy it matters
Reduced GCSWorsening brain function
Bradycardia + hypertensionLate sign of pressure rise
Unequal pupilsPossible herniation
Mass lesionNeeds urgent imaging and specialist input

Immediate principles
• ABC stabilization
• Elevate head when appropriate
• Urgent neuroimaging
• Treat cause

Do not miss

quiz.q: What late hemodynamic pattern may appear in raised ICP?
quiz.a: Bradycardia with hypertension as part of the Cushing response.

title: 😵 SYNCOPE APPROACH
content:
Syncope is transient loss of consciousness from brief cerebral hypoperfusion. The exam tests whether it is benign or dangerous.

High-risk clues
• Syncope during exertion
• Chest pain or palpitations
• Abnormal ECG
• Structural heart disease
• Family history of sudden death

CauseTypical clue
VasovagalStress, pain, prolonged standing
OrthostaticStanding up, volume loss, medications
CardiacSudden, exertional, palpitations, abnormal ECG

Basic workup
• History
• Orthostatic vitals
• ECG
• Focused examination

Decision line

quiz.q: What single test should almost every syncope patient get first?

title: 🔪 ACUTE APPENDICITIS
content:
Appendicitis is a common surgical emergency. The exam tests the pattern, not only the pain location.

Typical pattern
• Periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant
• Anorexia
• Nausea or vomiting
• Fever
• Localized tenderness

FeatureMeaning
Migratory painClassic clue
RLQ tendernessLocal peritoneal irritation
Rebound or guardingMore advanced inflammation
Perforation signsUrgent surgical concern

Management idea
Clinical suspicion first, then imaging when appropriate, and surgical review.

Classic progression

quiz.q: What pain migration pattern is classic for appendicitis?
quiz.a: From periumbilical pain to right lower quadrant pain.

title: 🧱 BOWEL OBSTRUCTION
content:
Bowel obstruction is a surgical emergency when strangulation or perforation is threatened.

Core features
• Colicky abdominal pain
• Vomiting
• Distension
• Obstipation

FeatureInterpretation
Colicky painMechanical obstruction clue
DistensionCommon in distal obstruction
No flatus or stoolObstipation
Fever, peritonitis, tachycardiaThink ischemia or perforation

Initial steps
• ABCs and fluids
• Decompression when indicated
• Urgent surgical assessment if complicated

Danger line

quiz.q: What four symptoms make up the classic bowel obstruction picture?
quiz.a: Colicky pain, vomiting, distension, and obstipation.

title: 🥵 ACUTE PANCREATITIS
content:
Acute pancreatitis usually presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back.

Common clues
• Epigastric pain to the back
• Nausea and vomiting
• Worse after eating
• Tender epigastrium

Common causes
• Gallstones
• Alcohol

TestWhy useful
LipaseSupports diagnosis
LFTsSuggest gallstone cause
ImagingAssess complications or cause

Initial treatment
• IV fluids
• Analgesia
• Supportive care

Exam pattern

quiz.q: What pain pattern is classic for acute pancreatitis?
quiz.a: Severe epigastric pain radiating to the back.

title: 🧠 MENINGITIS
content:
Meningitis is an emergency because delay in treatment increases morbidity and mortality.

Classic features
• Fever
• Headache
• Neck stiffness
• Photophobia
• Altered mental status may occur

ConcernAction
Suspected meningitisDo not delay empiric treatment
Shock or severe illnessResuscitate and treat immediately
Focal deficit / papilledemaConsider imaging before LP

Principles
• Blood cultures when possible
• Empiric antibiotics promptly
• Lumbar puncture when safe

Time-sensitive logic

quiz.q: What is the key management principle in suspected bacterial meningitis?

title: 👶 EPIGLOTTITIS VS CROUP
content:
These pediatric airway emergencies must be separated quickly.

FeatureEpiglottitisCroup
OnsetRapidUsually gradual
FeverHighLow-grade or absent
CoughUsually absentBarking cough
DroolingCommonUncommon
VoiceMuffledHoarse
Main riskSudden airway lossSubglottic swelling

Epiglottitis pearl
Do not agitate the child. Secure the airway with expert help.

Croup pearl
Dexamethasone is high yield.

Key separator

quiz.q: What symptom strongly points toward epiglottitis rather than croup?
quiz.a: Drooling with a toxic appearance and muffled voice.

title: 🍼 BRONCHIOLITIS
content:
Bronchiolitis is a common viral lower respiratory illness in infants, usually due to RSV.

Typical picture
• Runny nose first
• Cough
• Wheeze or crackles
• Increased work of breathing

Management pointHigh-yield note
Nasal suctionImportant supportive care
Oxygen if hypoxicAdmit if needed
Routine bronchodilatorsNot standard in classic bronchiolitis
HydrationMaintain intake

Admission clue
Hypoxia or significant work of breathing.

Typical sequence

quiz.q: What is the main treatment for bronchiolitis?
quiz.a: Supportive care, including nasal suction and oxygen if needed.

title: 💧 PEDIATRIC SEVERE DEHYDRATION
content:
In children, severe dehydration is recognized by perfusion failure and altered behavior, not only by dry mouth.

Severe signs
• Lethargy
• Poor perfusion
• Sunken eyes
• Markedly decreased urine output
• Tachycardia

SeverityExamples
MildThirst, slightly dry mucosa
ModerateSunken eyes, reduced urine
SevereLethargy, poor perfusion, shock signs

Management rule
Oral rehydration if able to drink and not in shock. IV fluids if severe or shocked.

Escalation logic

quiz.q: When should IV fluids be used in pediatric dehydration?
quiz.a: In severe dehydration or shock, or when oral rehydration is not feasible.

title: 🩸 ABRUPTION VS PREVIA
content:
Antepartum bleeding questions often ask you to separate placental abruption from placenta previa.

FeatureAbruptionPrevia
PainPainfulUsually painless
UterusTender, may be rigidUsually soft
BleedingMay be concealedVisible vaginal bleeding
Fetal distressCommonVariable

Exam pearl
Painful bleeding with a tender uterus strongly suggests abruption.

Safety point
Do not do a digital vaginal exam if placenta previa is suspected until placental location is known.

Rapid split

quiz.q: What bleeding pattern strongly suggests placental abruption?
quiz.a: Painful vaginal bleeding with a tender uterus.

title: 🤰 POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE
content:
Postpartum hemorrhage is a major obstetric emergency. Think causes systematically.

The 4 Ts
• Tone - uterine atony
• Tissue - retained products
• Trauma - laceration or rupture
• Thrombin - coagulopathy

CauseTypical clue
ToneBoggy uterus
TissueRetained placenta or fragments
TraumaFirm uterus but ongoing bleeding
ThrombinBleeding disorder pattern

Immediate priorities
• Resuscitation
• Uterine massage when atony suspected
• Identify cause rapidly

Think fast

quiz.q: What are the 4 Ts of postpartum hemorrhage?

title: 🩺 ECTOPIC PREGNANCY
content:
Ectopic pregnancy must be considered in any reproductive-age patient with pain and bleeding.

Classic clues
• Amenorrhea or missed period
• Lower abdominal pain
• Vaginal bleeding
• Shock if ruptured

ConcernWhy urgent
RuptureLife-threatening hemorrhage
Shoulder tip painMay suggest intraperitoneal bleeding
Positive pregnancy testImportant first clue

Key workup
• Pregnancy test
• Hemodynamic assessment
• Pelvic ultrasound when stable

Emergency logic

quiz.q: What diagnosis must be considered in a reproductive-age patient with abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding?

title: 🚑 TRAUMA PRIMARY RULE
content:
In trauma always treat life threats first.

Primary survey
A – Airway with cervical spine protection
B – Breathing
C – Circulation and hemorrhage control
D – Disability (GCS)
E – Exposure

Exam pearl
Do not send the unstable patient to CT before completing ABCDE.

quiz.q: What must always be completed before CT in trauma?

title: 🫀 CARDIAC TAMPONADE
content:
Tamponade occurs when fluid compresses the heart and blocks filling.

Classic clues
Hypotension
JVD
Muffled heart sounds

Other findings
Tachycardia
Pulsus paradoxus
Shock state

Immediate management
Pericardiocentesis.

quiz.q: What is the life-saving treatment for cardiac tamponade?

title: 🦴 COMPARTMENT SYNDROME
content:
Compartment syndrome causes muscle ischemia from high pressure inside a fascial compartment.

Early signs
Pain out of proportion
Pain with passive stretch
Tense compartment

Late signs
Paresthesia
Paralysis
Pulselessness

Treatment
Emergency fasciotomy.

quiz.q: What is the earliest important sign of compartment syndrome?
quiz.a: Pain out of proportion and pain with passive stretch.

title: 🔥 BURN DEPTH
content:
Burn questions often test depth recognition.

First degree
Red skin
No blisters
Painful

Second degree
Blisters
Moist
Very painful

Third degree
White or charred
Dry leathery skin
May be painless due to nerve destruction.

quiz.q: Which burn depth commonly has blisters?

title: 👶 FEBRILE SEIZURE
content:
Febrile seizure occurs in children with fever and no CNS infection.

Simple febrile seizure
Generalized
Less than 15 minutes
No recurrence in 24 hours

Management
Reassurance and evaluation of fever source.

quiz.q: What defines a simple febrile seizure?
quiz.a: Generalized seizure lasting less than 15 minutes and not recurring within 24 hours.

title: 🧠 STATUS EPILEPTICUS
content:
Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency.

Definition
Seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without recovery.

Immediate steps
Airway support
Check glucose

First medication
Benzodiazepine such as lorazepam.

quiz.q: What is the first medication used in status epilepticus?

title: 🫁 TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX
content:
Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening chest emergency.

Classic findings
Severe dyspnea
Hypotension
Tracheal deviation
Unilateral absent breath sounds

Treatment
Immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube.

quiz.q: Should you wait for chest X-ray before treating tension pneumothorax?

title: 🩸 SEPTIC SHOCK
content:
Septic shock occurs due to severe infection causing circulatory failure.

Clues
Hypotension
Tachycardia
Fever or hypothermia
Elevated lactate

Management
Early antibiotics
IV fluids
Source control.

quiz.q: What treatment should be given early in septic shock?

title: ❤️ ACUTE MI FIRST STEP
content:
Suspect myocardial infarction in patients with ischemic chest pain.

Symptoms
Chest pressure
Radiation to arm or jaw
Diaphoresis
Nausea

First investigation
ECG immediately.

quiz.q: What is the first investigation in suspected myocardial infarction?

title: 💊 OPIOID OVERDOSE
content:
Opioid overdose depresses the respiratory center.

Classic triad
Respiratory depression
Pinpoint pupils
Decreased consciousness

Treatment
Naloxone and airway support.

quiz.q: What medication reverses opioid overdose?

title: 🧠 ACUTE STROKE
content:
Stroke must be treated rapidly because neurons die quickly.

Typical symptoms
Facial droop
Arm weakness
Speech difficulty
Sudden vision loss

FAST rule
Face
Arm
Speech
Time

First step
Immediate non-contrast CT brain.

quiz.q: What imaging is required first in suspected stroke?
quiz.a: Non-contrast CT scan of the brain.

title: 🍬 DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS
content:
DKA results from insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia and ketone production.

Features
Hyperglycemia
Ketonemia
Metabolic acidosis

Symptoms
Polyuria
Abdominal pain
Kussmaul breathing

Management
IV fluids
Insulin therapy
Potassium monitoring.

quiz.q: What electrolyte must be closely monitored during DKA treatment?

title: ⚡ HYPOGLYCEMIA
content:
Hypoglycemia occurs when blood glucose drops below normal levels.

Symptoms
Sweating
Tremor
Confusion
Seizure if severe

Treatment
Immediate glucose administration.

quiz.q: What is the immediate treatment for severe symptomatic hypoglycemia?

title: 💥 AORTIC DISSECTION
content:
Aortic dissection occurs when a tear forms in the aortic wall.

Classic symptom
Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back.

Other clues
Pulse deficit
Blood pressure difference between arms

Management
Urgent imaging and blood pressure control.

quiz.q: What description of pain is classic for aortic dissection?
quiz.a: Sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back.

title: 🫁 PULMONARY EMBOLISM
content:
Pulmonary embolism is blockage of pulmonary arteries by thrombus.

Symptoms
Sudden dyspnea
Pleuritic chest pain
Tachycardia
Hypoxia

Diagnostic test
CT pulmonary angiography.

quiz.q: What imaging confirms pulmonary embolism?

title: 🩸 UPPER GI BLEED
content:
Upper gastrointestinal bleeding originates proximal to the ligament of Treitz.

Signs
Hematemesis
Melena
Hypotension if severe

Management priority
Resuscitation before endoscopy.

quiz.q: What must be done before endoscopy in unstable GI bleed?

title: ⚠️ ANAPHYLAXIS
content:
Anaphylaxis is a severe systemic allergic reaction.

Symptoms
Urticaria
Airway swelling
Hypotension

Immediate treatment
Intramuscular epinephrine.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis?

title: 🌬️ ACUTE ASTHMA
content:
Asthma exacerbation causes reversible airway obstruction.

Symptoms
Wheezing
Dyspnea
Chest tightness

Treatment
Short acting beta agonist such as salbutamol.

quiz.q: What is the first bronchodilator used in acute asthma?

title: 📉 HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY
content:
Hypertensive emergency occurs when severe blood pressure elevation causes organ damage.

Possible damage
Encephalopathy
Pulmonary edema
Renal failure

Management
Rapid blood pressure reduction with IV antihypertensives.

quiz.q: What differentiates hypertensive emergency from urgency?

title: 🧪 ORGANOPHOSPHATE POISONING
content:
Organophosphate poisoning causes cholinergic excess.

Symptoms
Salivation
Lacrimation
Urination
Diarrhea
Bronchospasm

Treatment
Atropine and pralidoxime.

quiz.q: What is the primary antidote for organophosphate poisoning?

title: 🚬 CARBON MONOXIDE POISONING
content:
Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with very high affinity and prevents oxygen delivery.

Clues
Headache
Dizziness
Nausea
Confusion
Multiple people affected in the same environment

Treatment
High-flow oxygen immediately.

quiz.q: What is the first treatment for carbon monoxide poisoning?

title: 💥 SUBARACHNOID HEMORRHAGE
content:
Subarachnoid hemorrhage is usually due to ruptured berry aneurysm.

Classic description
Worst headache of life
Thunderclap onset

Other symptoms
Neck stiffness
Vomiting
Photophobia

First test
CT head.

quiz.q: What is the classic headache description in subarachnoid hemorrhage?

title: 🚗 FAST ULTRASOUND
content:
FAST exam detects free fluid in trauma patients.

Exam areas
Pericardium
Right upper quadrant
Left upper quadrant
Pelvis

Purpose
Rapid detection of internal bleeding.

quiz.q: What does FAST ultrasound detect in trauma?

title: 🔥 BURN RESUSCITATION
content:
Major burns require aggressive fluid replacement.

Problem
Massive capillary leak

Monitoring
Urine output is used to assess adequate resuscitation.

quiz.q: What parameter is used to monitor burn resuscitation?

title: 🌡️ HEAT STROKE
content:
Heat stroke is hyperthermia with CNS dysfunction.

Symptoms
Very high temperature
Confusion
Hot skin

Treatment
Rapid cooling.

quiz.q: What differentiates heat stroke from heat exhaustion?

title: 🥶 HYPOTHERMIA
content:
Hypothermia occurs when core temperature falls below 35°C.

Symptoms
Shivering
Confusion
Bradycardia

Management
Rewarming and monitoring for arrhythmia.

quiz.q: What core temperature defines hypothermia?

title: 🧊 TESTICULAR TORSION
content:
Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency.

Signs
Sudden severe scrotal pain
High-riding testicle
Absent cremasteric reflex

Management
Immediate surgery.

quiz.q: What reflex is usually absent in testicular torsion?

title: 🩸 OVARIAN TORSION
content:
Ovarian torsion occurs when the ovary twists around its vascular pedicle.

Symptoms
Sudden pelvic pain
Nausea
Vomiting

Management
Urgent surgical evaluation.

quiz.q: What symptom combination strongly suggests ovarian torsion?
quiz.a: Sudden pelvic pain with nausea and vomiting.

title: 🪨 RENAL COLIC
content:
Renal colic occurs due to ureteric stone obstruction.

Symptoms
Severe flank pain
Radiation to groin
Hematuria

Exam clue
Patient cannot sit still due to pain.

quiz.q: What classic pain radiation occurs in renal colic?
quiz.a: Flank pain radiating to the groin.

title: 🚫 URINARY RETENTION
content:
Acute urinary retention is inability to void with painful bladder distension.

Symptoms
Suprapubic pain
Distended bladder

Treatment
Immediate bladder catheterization.

quiz.q: What is the immediate treatment for acute urinary retention?

title: ⚡ HYPERKALEMIA ECG
content:
Hyperkalemia causes dangerous cardiac conduction abnormalities.

ECG findings
Peaked T waves
Wide QRS

Treatment priority
IV calcium to stabilize myocardium.

quiz.q: What drug stabilizes the heart in severe hyperkalemia?

title: 💧 HYPONATREMIA
content:
Severe hyponatremia can cause neurologic dysfunction.

Symptoms
Confusion
Seizures
Coma

Management
Careful sodium correction.

quiz.q: What severe symptom can occur in hyponatremia?

title: 🦋 THYROID STORM
content:
Thyroid storm is severe thyrotoxicosis with systemic instability.

Symptoms
Fever
Tachycardia
Delirium

Treatment
Beta blockers and antithyroid therapy.

quiz.q: What symptom triad suggests thyroid storm?

title: 🧂 ADRENAL CRISIS
content:
Adrenal crisis is acute cortisol deficiency.

Symptoms
Hypotension
Vomiting
Weakness

Treatment
IV steroids and fluids.

quiz.q: What is the immediate treatment for adrenal crisis?

title: 👶 CHILD DEHYDRATION
content:
Children with dehydration show decreased body fluid volume.

Signs
Dry mucosa
Sunken eyes
Reduced urine

Treatment
Oral rehydration solution.

quiz.q: What is first-line treatment for mild pediatric dehydration?

title: 🤒 FEBRILE SEIZURE
content:
Febrile seizure occurs in young children with fever.

Features
Generalized seizure
Short duration

Management
Reassurance and treat fever cause.

quiz.q: What type of seizure usually occurs in febrile seizures?

title: 🤰 ECLAMPSIA
content:
Eclampsia is seizure in a patient with preeclampsia.

Signs
Hypertension
Seizure

Treatment
Magnesium sulfate.

quiz.q: What medication treats eclamptic seizures?

title: 💊 ACETAMINOPHEN OVERDOSE
content:
Acetaminophen overdose can cause severe liver failure.

Key point
Symptoms may be mild early.

Antidote
N-acetylcysteine.

quiz.q: What antidote treats acetaminophen overdose?

title: ⚡ CARDIAC ARREST RHYTHMS
content:
Cardiac arrest rhythms are classified as shockable or non-shockable.

Shockable
Ventricular fibrillation
Pulseless ventricular tachycardia

Non-shockable
Asystole
PEA

quiz.q: Which cardiac arrest rhythm is shockable?

title: 🫀 HEMORRHAGIC SHOCK
content:
Hemorrhagic shock results from severe blood loss.

Signs
Tachycardia
Cold skin
Hypotension

Priority
Control bleeding and restore circulation.

quiz.q: What is the first priority in hemorrhagic shock?

title: 🧠 MENINGITIS RED FLAGS
content:
Meningitis is infection of the meninges and can progress rapidly.

Classic triad
Fever
Neck stiffness
Altered mental status

Management
Start empiric antibiotics immediately after blood cultures.

quiz.q: What is the first treatment step when meningitis is strongly suspected?

title: 🧠 ENCEPHALITIS
content:
Encephalitis is inflammation of brain parenchyma, often viral.

Symptoms
Fever
Altered consciousness
Seizures

Key treatment
IV acyclovir if HSV is suspected.

quiz.q: What drug is used for suspected HSV encephalitis?

title: 🧠 GUILLAIN-BARRÉ
content:
Guillain-Barré syndrome is acute immune neuropathy causing ascending weakness.

Signs
Ascending paralysis
Areflexia

Treatment
IVIG or plasmapheresis.

quiz.q: What reflex finding is typical in Guillain-Barré?

title: ⚡ MYASTHENIC CRISIS
content:
Myasthenic crisis causes respiratory failure due to severe muscle weakness.

Symptoms
Respiratory weakness
Bulbar weakness

Management
Airway support and IVIG or plasmapheresis.

quiz.q: What complication defines myasthenic crisis?
quiz.a: Respiratory failure due to muscle weakness.

title: 🧠 CAUDA EQUINA
content:
Cauda equina syndrome occurs when nerve roots are compressed in the lumbar spine.

Symptoms
Saddle anesthesia
Urinary retention
Bilateral leg weakness

Management
Urgent surgical decompression.

quiz.q: What urinary symptom suggests cauda equina syndrome?

title: 🧠 SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION
content:
Spinal cord compression causes neurologic deficits below the lesion.

Symptoms
Back pain
Weakness
Sensory loss

Management
Urgent imaging and decompression.

quiz.q: What imaging is used for suspected spinal cord compression?

title: 🍽️ ACUTE PANCREATITIS
content:
Pancreatitis is inflammation of the pancreas.

Symptoms
Epigastric pain
Radiation to back
Nausea

Common causes
Gallstones
Alcohol.

quiz.q: What two causes account for most pancreatitis cases?

title: 🚫 BOWEL OBSTRUCTION
content:
Bowel obstruction blocks intestinal passage.

Symptoms
Abdominal pain
Vomiting
Abdominal distension

Management
NG tube decompression and surgical evaluation.

quiz.q: What symptom triad suggests bowel obstruction?

title: 🤰 ECTOPIC PREGNANCY
content:
Ectopic pregnancy occurs when implantation occurs outside the uterus.

Classic triad
Amenorrhea
Abdominal pain
Vaginal bleeding

Diagnosis
Ultrasound and beta-hCG.

quiz.q: What is the classic triad of ectopic pregnancy?

title: ❤️ PERICARDITIS
content:
Pericarditis causes inflammation of the pericardial sac.

Symptoms
Sharp chest pain
Better when leaning forward

ECG finding
Diffuse ST elevation.

quiz.q: What body position relieves pericarditis pain?

title: 🫁 COPD EXACERBATION
content:
COPD exacerbation causes worsening airflow obstruction.

Symptoms
Dyspnea
Cough
Sputum increase

Treatment
Bronchodilators and steroids.

quiz.q: What medication class is used in COPD exacerbation?

title: 🫁 PNEUMONIA
content:
Pneumonia is infection of lung parenchyma.

Symptoms
Fever
Cough
Dyspnea

Diagnosis
Chest X-ray.

quiz.q: What imaging confirms pneumonia?

title: 🦠 SEPSIS
content:
Sepsis occurs when infection causes systemic organ dysfunction.

Signs
Tachycardia
Hypotension
Fever

Management
Early antibiotics and IV fluids.

quiz.q: What treatment must be given early in sepsis?

title: ⚡ ATRIAL FIBRILLATION
content:
Atrial fibrillation is irregular atrial rhythm.

Features
Irregularly irregular pulse
No P waves

Complication
Stroke risk.

quiz.q: What pulse finding occurs in atrial fibrillation?

title: ⚡ SUPRAVENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA
content:
SVT causes rapid narrow complex tachycardia.

Symptoms
Palpitations
Dizziness

Treatment
Vagal maneuvers or adenosine.

quiz.q: What drug terminates SVT acutely?

title: 🫁 FLAIL CHEST
content:
Flail chest occurs when multiple ribs fracture in two places.

Finding
Paradoxical chest movement.

Management
Pain control and respiratory support.

quiz.q: What physical sign indicates flail chest?

title: 🧠 EPIDURAL HEMATOMA
content:
Epidural hematoma usually results from middle meningeal artery rupture.

Clue
Lucid interval after trauma.

Treatment
Urgent neurosurgery.

quiz.q: What classic history suggests epidural hematoma?

title: 🧠 SUBDURAL HEMATOMA
content:
Subdural hematoma occurs due to tearing of bridging veins.

Common in
Elderly or alcoholics.

CT finding
Crescent-shaped bleed.

quiz.q: What CT shape occurs in subdural hematoma?

title: 🧠 SEROTONIN SYNDROME
content:
Serotonin syndrome results from excess serotonin activity.

Symptoms
Hyperreflexia
Clonus
Agitation

Treatment
Stop offending drug and supportive care.

quiz.q: What neuromuscular sign is typical in serotonin syndrome?

title: ⚠️ NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME
content:
NMS occurs due to dopamine blockade from antipsychotics.

Symptoms
Muscle rigidity
Fever
Autonomic instability

Treatment
Stop antipsychotic and give supportive care.

quiz.q: What muscle finding occurs in neuroleptic malignant syndrome?

title: 🩸 DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS
content:
DKA is a life-threatening complication of diabetes with insulin deficiency.

Symptoms
Polyuria
Abdominal pain
Kussmaul breathing

Labs
High glucose
Ketones
Metabolic acidosis

Treatment
IV fluids and insulin.

quiz.q: What breathing pattern is typical in DKA?

title: 🍬 HYPEROSMOLAR HYPERGLYCEMIC STATE
content:
HHS occurs mainly in type 2 diabetes with severe hyperglycemia and dehydration.

Features
Very high glucose
No significant ketosis

Treatment
IV fluids then insulin.

quiz.q: What key lab difference separates HHS from DKA?

title: ⚡ HYPOGLYCEMIA
content:
Hypoglycemia occurs when blood glucose drops below normal levels.

Symptoms
Sweating
Tremor
Confusion

Treatment
Give glucose immediately.

quiz.q: What is the immediate treatment for severe hypoglycemia?

title: 🍽️ APPENDICITIS
content:
Appendicitis is inflammation of the appendix.

Classic pain pattern
Pain starts periumbilical then moves to right lower quadrant.

Treatment
Surgical removal.

quiz.q: Where does appendicitis pain typically localize later?

title: 🟢 CHOLECYSTITIS
content:
Cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder usually due to gallstones.

Symptoms
Right upper quadrant pain
Fever
Nausea

Diagnosis
Ultrasound.

quiz.q: What imaging test is first line for cholecystitis?

title: 🦵 DEEP VEIN THROMBOSIS
content:
DVT is clot formation in deep veins of the leg.

Symptoms
Leg swelling
Pain
Warmth

Diagnosis
Doppler ultrasound.

quiz.q: What imaging confirms DVT?

title: 🫁 PULMONARY EMBOLISM
content:
Pulmonary embolism occurs when a clot travels to pulmonary arteries.

Symptoms
Sudden dyspnea
Chest pain
Tachycardia

Diagnosis
CT pulmonary angiography.

quiz.q: What imaging confirms pulmonary embolism?

title: 🧠 ISCHEMIC STROKE
content:
Ischemic stroke occurs when blood flow to brain is blocked.

Symptoms
Sudden weakness
Speech difficulty

Treatment window
Thrombolysis if early.

quiz.q: What drug class is used for early ischemic stroke treatment?

title: 🧠 TRANSIENT ISCHEMIC ATTACK
content:
TIA is temporary neurologic deficit without permanent damage.

Key point
Symptoms resolve within 24 hours.

Importance
Warning sign for stroke.

quiz.q: How long do TIA symptoms last?

title: 🫁 STATUS ASTHMATICUS
content:
Status asthmaticus is severe asthma attack not responding to usual therapy.

Symptoms
Severe dyspnea
Silent chest

Treatment
Bronchodilators and steroids.

quiz.q: What alarming sign may occur in severe asthma attack?

title: 🩸 ANEMIA
content:
Anemia is reduced hemoglobin causing decreased oxygen delivery.

Symptoms
Fatigue
Pallor
Shortness of breath.

quiz.q: What symptom commonly occurs in anemia?

title: 🩸 IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA
content:
Iron deficiency anemia is the most common anemia worldwide.

Features
Low ferritin
Microcytic RBC.

quiz.q: What RBC size occurs in iron deficiency anemia?

title: 🧬 SICKLE CELL CRISIS
content:
Sickle cell crisis causes painful vaso-occlusion due to sickled RBC.

Symptoms
Severe pain episodes
Anemia

Management
Pain control and hydration.

quiz.q: What triggers vaso-occlusion in sickle cell disease?
quiz.a: Sickled red blood cells blocking vessels.

title: 🦠 HIV INFECTION
content:
HIV destroys CD4 T lymphocytes leading to immunodeficiency.

Transmission
Sexual contact
Blood exposure

Treatment
Antiretroviral therapy.

quiz.q: What immune cell is targeted by HIV?

title: 🦠 CELLULITIS
content:
Cellulitis is bacterial infection of skin and subcutaneous tissue.

Symptoms
Redness
Warmth
Swelling.

quiz.q: What skin findings suggest cellulitis?

title: 🦠 NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
content:
Necrotizing fasciitis is rapidly spreading soft tissue infection.

Clue
Pain out of proportion to exam.

Treatment
Emergency surgery.

quiz.q: What key symptom suggests necrotizing fasciitis?

title: 🧠 MAJOR DEPRESSION
content:
Major depressive disorder causes persistent low mood and loss of interest.

Symptoms
Low mood
Anhedonia
Sleep disturbance.

quiz.q: What symptom refers to loss of pleasure?

title: ⚡ BIPOLAR MANIA
content:
Mania is elevated mood with increased activity and decreased need for sleep.

Symptoms
Grandiosity
Rapid speech
Impulsivity.

quiz.q: What sleep pattern occurs in mania?

title: 🧠 SCHIZOPHRENIA
content:
Schizophrenia causes psychosis and disorganized thinking.

Symptoms
Hallucinations
Delusions
Disorganized speech.

quiz.q: What perception disturbance occurs in schizophrenia?

title: 🚑 ANAPHYLAXIS
content:
Anaphylaxis is severe allergic reaction causing airway and circulatory collapse.

Symptoms
Urticaria
Hypotension
Airway swelling

Treatment
Immediate epinephrine.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis?
